texts.orig: Simulations of Membrane Gas Separation: Chemical Solvent Absorption Hybrid Plants for Pre- and Post-Combustion Carbon Capture
texts.text: Simulations of Membrane Gas Separation: Chemical Solvent Absorption Hybrid Plants for Pre- and Post-Combustion Carbon Capture

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Colin A. Scholes, Clare J. Anderson, Robyn Cuthbertson, Geoff W. Stevens, and Sandra E. Kentish
texts.text: Colin A. Scholes, Clare J. Anderson, Robyn Cuthbertson, Geoff W. Stevens, and Sandra E. Kentish

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cooperative Research Centre for Greenhouse Gas Technologies (CO2CRC), Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, University of Melbourne, VIC, Australia
texts.text: Cooperative Research Centre for Greenhouse Gas Technologies (CO2CRC), Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, University of Melbourne, VIC, Australia

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solvent absorption and membrane gas separation are two carbon capture technologies that show great potential for reducing emissions from stationary sources such as power plants. Here, plants combining chemical solvent absorption and membrane gas separation are considered for post-combustion capture as well as pre-combustion capture. In all ASPEN HYSYS simulations the membrane stage initially concentrates CO2 into either the permeate or the retentate stream, which is then passed to a monoethanolamine (MEA) based solvent absorption process. In particular, postcombustion capture scenarios examined a membrane that is selective for CO2 against N2, while for the pre-combustion scenario a H2selective membrane was studied. It was found the energy demand of the combined hybrid plant was always more than that of a stand alone MEA solvent process. This was mainly due to the need to generate a pressure driving force upstream of the membrane in the post-combustion scenario or to recompress downstream gas streams in the pre-combustion scenarios. For both scenarios concentrating the CO2 in the feed to the solvent system reduced the absorber column height and diameter, which could represent a CAPEX saving for the hybrid plant, dependent upon the membrane price. The use of a hydrogen selective membrane downstream of an oxygen fired gasifier was identified as the most prospective scenario, as it led to significant reductions in absorber size, for a relatively small membrane area and energy penalty.
texts.text: Solvent absorption and membrane gas separation are two carbon capture technologies that show great potential for reducing emissions from stationary sources such as power plants. Here, plants combining chemical solvent absorption and membrane gas separation are considered for post-combustion capture as well as pre-combustion capture. In all ASPEN HYSYS simulations the membrane stage initially concentrates CO2 into either the permeate or the retentate stream, which is then passed to a monoethanolamine (MEA) based solvent absorption process. In particular, postcombustion capture scenarios examined a membrane that is selective for CO2 against N2, while for the pre-combustion scenario a H2selective membrane was studied. It was found the energy demand of the combined hybrid plant was always more than that of a stand alone MEA solvent process. This was mainly due to the need to generate a pressure driving force upstream of the membrane in the post-combustion scenario or to recompress downstream gas streams in the pre-combustion scenarios. For both scenarios concentrating the CO2 in the feed to the solvent system reduced the absorber column height and diameter, which could represent a CAPEX saving for the hybrid plant, dependent upon the membrane price. The use of a hydrogen selective membrane downstream of an oxygen fired gasifier was identified as the most prospective scenario, as it led to significant reductions in absorber size, for a relatively small membrane area and energy penalty.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: INTRODUCTION
texts.text: INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anthropogenic climate change is a significant environmental and economic issue which is associated with the emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from fossil fuels. One of the major sources of CO2 is the combustion of coal for power generation. Carbon capture and storage (CCS) is a proposed strategy to reduce carbon emissions from coal-fired power
texts.text: Anthropogenic climate change is a significant environmental and economic issue which is associated with the emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from fossil fuels. One of the major sources of CO2 is the combustion of coal for power generation. Carbon capture and storage (CCS) is a proposed strategy to reduce carbon emissions from coal-fired power

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: Received 6 January 2013; accepted 3 April 2013.
texts.text: Received 6 January 2013; accepted 3 April 2013.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Address correspondence to Sandra E. Kentish, Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia. E-mail: sandraek@unimelb. edu.au stations, although carbon capture has been shown to be expensive (1). There are three carbon capture strategies for a power station (1): post-combustion, which involves separating CO2 from the flue gas after the combustion process; pre-combustion, which involves gasification of the coal into syngas and separation of CO2 from hydrogen before the combustion process; and oxy-fuel combustion, where oxygen is separated from air prior to combustion. Pre-combustion can be further classified into an oxygen or air blown gasification process, the latter producing a syngas with a significant nitrogen content. The target for carbon capture processes, set by the USA Department of Energy, is to recover 90% of the CO2 in the flue gas feed and produce a product purity of 95% CO2 for storage (2).
texts.text: Address correspondence to Sandra E. Kentish, Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia. E-mail: sandraek@unimelb. edu.au stations, although carbon capture has been shown to be expensive (1). There are three carbon capture strategies for a power station (1): post-combustion, which involves separating CO2 from the flue gas after the combustion process; pre-combustion, which involves gasification of the coal into syngas and separation of CO2 from hydrogen before the combustion process; and oxy-fuel combustion, where oxygen is separated from air prior to combustion. Pre-combustion can be further classified into an oxygen or air blown gasification process, the latter producing a syngas with a significant nitrogen content. The target for carbon capture processes, set by the USA Department of Energy, is to recover 90% of the CO2 in the flue gas feed and produce a product purity of 95% CO2 for storage (2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of the various carbon capture technologies available, reversible chemical solvent absorption and membrane gas separation have both been proven commercially. In reversible chemical solvent absorption, the flue gas is fed into an absorption column and contacted with a chemical solvent that reacts with CO2 (3). This removes the CO2 from the flue gas and the clean gas exits the top of the absorption column. The CO2 rich solvent is then fed into a regenerator column, where heat is applied to the solvent and the reverse reaction occurs, releasing CO2. The CO2 gas leaves the top of the regenerator column in a highly pure state and the regenerated lean solvent is recycled back to the absorption column. This process is able to recover almost all of the CO2 from the flue gas and produce a high purity CO2 stream. However, the large scale of the absorption and regenerator columns required for CCS applications, as well the high energy demand of the reboiler in the regenerator column detracts from the economic and technical feasibility of this process.
texts.text: Of the various carbon capture technologies available, reversible chemical solvent absorption and membrane gas separation have both been proven commercially. In reversible chemical solvent absorption, the flue gas is fed into an absorption column and contacted with a chemical solvent that reacts with CO2 (3). This removes the CO2 from the flue gas and the clean gas exits the top of the absorption column. The CO2 rich solvent is then fed into a regenerator column, where heat is applied to the solvent and the reverse reaction occurs, releasing CO2. The CO2 gas leaves the top of the regenerator column in a highly pure state and the regenerated lean solvent is recycled back to the absorption column. This process is able to recover almost all of the CO2 from the flue gas and produce a high purity CO2 stream. However, the large scale of the absorption and regenerator columns required for CCS applications, as well the high energy demand of the reboiler in the regenerator column detracts from the economic and technical feasibility of this process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation is conceptually a simple process where a semi-selective membrane allows one or more gases to pass through at the expense of other gases present in the flue gas (4). This enables CO2 to be concentrated on one side of the membrane. Membranes have the advantage of a smaller footprint and easy operation.
texts.text: Membrane gas separation is conceptually a simple process where a semi-selective membrane allows one or more gases to pass through at the expense of other gases present in the flue gas (4). This enables CO2 to be concentrated on one side of the membrane. Membranes have the advantage of a smaller footprint and easy operation.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: CARBON CAPTURE TECHNOLOGIES
texts.text: CARBON CAPTURE TECHNOLOGIES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, to achieve good purity it is necessary to have multiple membrane stages, and given the need to have a large pressure difference across the membrane significant compression or vacuum requirements are necessary.
texts.text: However, to achieve good purity it is necessary to have multiple membrane stages, and given the need to have a large pressure difference across the membrane significant compression or vacuum requirements are necessary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Importantly, membrane gas separation and reversible solvent absorption technologies have complementary advantages and disadvantages. For example, membrane gas separation is able to effectively concentrate CO2 but has difficulty reaching the high purity required, whereas solvent absorption is able to provide the required purity, but has a significant equipment footprint when handling dilute CO2 feeds. A number of studies on hybrid processes for post-combustion capture have indicated that combining complementary capture technologies can produce a more competitive process (5-10). Therefore, this work examines a combination of membrane gas separation and reversible chemical solvent absorption in series as a hybrid process and studies the viability of this approach for post-combustion as well as oxygen and air blown pre-combustion scenarios. The solvent process is based on 28 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The post-combustion scenario examines a CO2-selective membrane that enriches the permeate stream that feeds the solvent process. In the pre-combustion scenarios, a H2-selective membrane is investigated. The former concentrates CO2 in the permeate stream that feeds the solvent process, while the latter permeates H2, leaving a CO2-rich retentate stream at pressure for downstream solvent absorption.
texts.text: Importantly, membrane gas separation and reversible solvent absorption technologies have complementary advantages and disadvantages. For example, membrane gas separation is able to effectively concentrate CO2 but has difficulty reaching the high purity required, whereas solvent absorption is able to provide the required purity, but has a significant equipment footprint when handling dilute CO2 feeds. A number of studies on hybrid processes for post-combustion capture have indicated that combining complementary capture technologies can produce a more competitive process (5-10). Therefore, this work examines a combination of membrane gas separation and reversible chemical solvent absorption in series as a hybrid process and studies the viability of this approach for post-combustion as well as oxygen and air blown pre-combustion scenarios. The solvent process is based on 28 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The post-combustion scenario examines a CO2-selective membrane that enriches the permeate stream that feeds the solvent process. In the pre-combustion scenarios, a H2-selective membrane is investigated. The former concentrates CO2 in the permeate stream that feeds the solvent process, while the latter permeates H2, leaving a CO2-rich retentate stream at pressure for downstream solvent absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simulations of these hybrid processes are compared with a base case of MEA solvent absorption, the current commercial benchmark. In particular, the relative change in energy demand and CAPEX are considered in detail to enable the viability of these hybrid processes to be evaluated.
texts.text: Simulations of these hybrid processes are compared with a base case of MEA solvent absorption, the current commercial benchmark. In particular, the relative change in energy demand and CAPEX are considered in detail to enable the viability of these hybrid processes to be evaluated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: METHODOLOGY
texts.text: METHODOLOGY

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All simulations were undertaken using the Aspen HYSYS 1 software package, version 7.1, using the PengRobinson fluid package for the membrane process and the Amine package for the MEA solvent process, with the Kent-Eisenburg thermodynamic model for the aqueous amine phase (AspenTech, USA). The solvent process operations were based on existing units in the HYSYS software package utilizing the modified HYSIM method with adaptive damping to assist in calculation convergence (11). The MEA feed concentration was 28 wt%. This solvent concentration is a little below the 30 wt% considered in most studies (12) but was chosen to ensure the MEA concentration remains below 30 wt% throughout the simulation, especially in the regenerator column, as this is the limit of the Aspen HYSYS Amine package. The membrane simulation used an in-house module specifically designed for gas separation processes, based on mass transfer equations for cross-flow and counter-flow configurations (13). The membrane separation process is broken down into
texts.text: All simulations were undertaken using the Aspen HYSYS 1 software package, version 7.1, using the PengRobinson fluid package for the membrane process and the Amine package for the MEA solvent process, with the Kent-Eisenburg thermodynamic model for the aqueous amine phase (AspenTech, USA). The solvent process operations were based on existing units in the HYSYS software package utilizing the modified HYSIM method with adaptive damping to assist in calculation convergence (11). The MEA feed concentration was 28 wt%. This solvent concentration is a little below the 30 wt% considered in most studies (12) but was chosen to ensure the MEA concentration remains below 30 wt% throughout the simulation, especially in the regenerator column, as this is the limit of the Aspen HYSYS Amine package. The membrane simulation used an in-house module specifically designed for gas separation processes, based on mass transfer equations for cross-flow and counter-flow configurations (13). The membrane separation process is broken down into

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 100 discrete stages, where the mass balance is determined iteratively for each stage.
texts.text: 100 discrete stages, where the mass balance is determined iteratively for each stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To provide a common basis for comparison, both the base case and the hybrid processes are specified to achieve a product purity of at least 95% CO2 and to capture 90% of the CO2 in the feed stream. In the hybrid processes, the unrecovered carbon dioxide is split equally between the membrane and solvent operations. For the solvent absorption process, the regenerator reboiler is set to operate at 115 kPaa and the regenerator condenser is set to operate at 40  C. The liquid to gas flowrate ratio is initially set at 5, with some variation as a result of optimization of each case. The membrane active layer thickness is set at 0.1 m m and a cross flow pressure drop of 10 kPa is assumed. The membrane module is operated in counter-current flow, except for the post-combustion capture scenario, where cross-current flow is also investigated. Existing spiral wound membrane modules operate in a cross-flow mode and it is likely that this will be the module configuration in first generation post-combustion capture plants.
texts.text: To provide a common basis for comparison, both the base case and the hybrid processes are specified to achieve a product purity of at least 95% CO2 and to capture 90% of the CO2 in the feed stream. In the hybrid processes, the unrecovered carbon dioxide is split equally between the membrane and solvent operations. For the solvent absorption process, the regenerator reboiler is set to operate at 115 kPaa and the regenerator condenser is set to operate at 40  C. The liquid to gas flowrate ratio is initially set at 5, with some variation as a result of optimization of each case. The membrane active layer thickness is set at 0.1 m m and a cross flow pressure drop of 10 kPa is assumed. The membrane module is operated in counter-current flow, except for the post-combustion capture scenario, where cross-current flow is also investigated. Existing spiral wound membrane modules operate in a cross-flow mode and it is likely that this will be the module configuration in first generation post-combustion capture plants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All other process units are operated using default HYSYS parameters, with pressure drop across heat exchangers assumed to be 40 kPa, temperature losses negligible and pumps and compressors operating at 75% efficiency. CAPEX estimates for the solvent process where undertaken using the Aspen Economic Evaluator package, 2008 1st Quarter, with default parameters.
texts.text: All other process units are operated using default HYSYS parameters, with pressure drop across heat exchangers assumed to be 40 kPa, temperature losses negligible and pumps and compressors operating at 75% efficiency. CAPEX estimates for the solvent process where undertaken using the Aspen Economic Evaluator package, 2008 1st Quarter, with default parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The post-combustion flue gas flowrate and composition is provided in Table 1, based on a 500 MW coal-fired power station in Victoria, Australia (14). The conditions are those that are experienced after the flue gas has gone through a direct contact cooler to remove ash, resulting in low temperature and high water content.
texts.text: The post-combustion flue gas flowrate and composition is provided in Table 1, based on a 500 MW coal-fired power station in Victoria, Australia (14). The conditions are those that are experienced after the flue gas has gone through a direct contact cooler to remove ash, resulting in low temperature and high water content.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membrane. To create the pressure drop across the membrane unit a vacuum pump is used to generate a vacuum of 16 kPaa in the permeate stream, which has been shown to be the most economical approach for post-combustion membrane separation (15). To ensure flue gas passes through the membrane a blower exists on the feed gas. To allow the option of increased pressure difference across the membrane, the blower and vacuum pump can be altered to a compressor only to enable a total membrane driving force of up to 400 kPa. The membrane area and stage-cut is varied to ensure that 95% of the CO2 in the feed stream separates into the permeate stream. The CO2 rich permeate stream then enters the MEA solvent process, where the absorber operates at 50  C. The CO2rich solvent leaving the absorber passes through a cross heat exchanger before entering the regenerator, which operates at 115 kPaa. The lean solvent is then returned to the absorber, via the cross heat exchanger and a second heat exchanger to lower the solvent to absorber temperature. The addition of make-up water and MEA counters that lost in the exit gases from the absorber and regenerator columns.
texts.text: membrane. To create the pressure drop across the membrane unit a vacuum pump is used to generate a vacuum of 16 kPaa in the permeate stream, which has been shown to be the most economical approach for post-combustion membrane separation (15). To ensure flue gas passes through the membrane a blower exists on the feed gas. To allow the option of increased pressure difference across the membrane, the blower and vacuum pump can be altered to a compressor only to enable a total membrane driving force of up to 400 kPa. The membrane area and stage-cut is varied to ensure that 95% of the CO2 in the feed stream separates into the permeate stream. The CO2 rich permeate stream then enters the MEA solvent process, where the absorber operates at 50  C. The CO2rich solvent leaving the absorber passes through a cross heat exchanger before entering the regenerator, which operates at 115 kPaa. The lean solvent is then returned to the absorber, via the cross heat exchanger and a second heat exchanger to lower the solvent to absorber temperature. The addition of make-up water and MEA counters that lost in the exit gases from the absorber and regenerator columns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For this post-combustion process, the membrane gas separation process variables are the CO2 permeability and the CO2 = N2 selectivity. To simplify the simulation it is assumed that the CO2 = O2 selectivity is equal to the CO2 = N2 selectivity. While this is somewhat different from an actual polymeric membrane the low O2 content relative to N2 means this has little influence on the results. The H2O = CO2 selectivity is assumed to be 100, which is in keeping with the literature for polymeric membranes (16). The MEA solvent base case is undertaken at the same temperature and pressure conditions as the hybrid process, yielding a reboiler duty of 4 MJ = kg of CO2, which is in agreement with reported energy demand for existing MEA processes (12,17-19). MEA solvent absorption is applied here because it is the most commercially successful solvent for CO2 separation. Alternative solvent systems, such as Fluor Econamine (20), Mitsubishi KS-Solvents (21), and Siemens AAS (22) can achieve lower energy demands, in the range

While this is somewhat different from an actual polymeric membrane the low O2 content relative to N2 means this has little influence on the results. The H2O = CO2 selectivity is assumed to be 100, which is in keeping with the literature for polymeric membranes (16). The MEA solvent base case is undertaken at the same temperature and pressure conditions as the hybrid process, yielding a reboiler duty of 4 MJ = kg of CO2, which is in agreement with reported energy demand for existing MEA processes (12,17-19). MEA solvent absorption is applied here because it is the most commercially successful solvent for CO2 separation. texts.orig: In the pre-combustion scenario, H2 is recovered in the permeate stream, with the retentate stream fed to the MEAsolvent plant. The absorber is operated at 2500 kPaa. The H2 permeability and the H2 = CO2 selectivity are the variables. In this case, the H2O permeability is assumed equal to the H2 permeability and the N2 and CO permeabilities are assumed equal, with the H2 = N2 selectivity 10 times the H2 = CO2 selectivity. In the case of an air-blown gasifier, the exit gas from the absorber, which is mainly

texts.text: CARBON CAPTURE TECHNOLOGIES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: N2, is used as a sweep gas in the membrane unit to maximize the H2 flux (Fig. 2). The sweep gas loses pressure as it passes through the membrane unit at 2200 kPa, and therefore to enable comparison with the base case the permeate stream is recompressed to the absorber exit pressure.
texts.text: N2, is used as a sweep gas in the membrane unit to maximize the H2 flux (Fig. 2). The sweep gas loses pressure as it passes through the membrane unit at 2200 kPa, and therefore to enable comparison with the base case the permeate stream is recompressed to the absorber exit pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The base case for the pre-combustion scenario operates at high pressure (2500 kPaa) to maximize separation. The rich MEA solvent leaving the absorber is depressurized to 115 kPaa to maximize desorption and minimize reboiler energy demand. The energy demand of this base case is 4.5 MJ = kg of CO2, which is higher than that for the post-combustion scenario due to the high pressure of the absorber and differences in the water load in the regenerator. Physical solvents, such as Purisol and Selexol, achieve much lower energy demands than this for pre-combustion capture, and hybrid processes of membranes and physical solvents have been reported elsewhere (26).
texts.text: The base case for the pre-combustion scenario operates at high pressure (2500 kPaa) to maximize separation. The rich MEA solvent leaving the absorber is depressurized to 115 kPaa to maximize desorption and minimize reboiler energy demand. The energy demand of this base case is 4.5 MJ = kg of CO2, which is higher than that for the post-combustion scenario due to the high pressure of the absorber and differences in the water load in the regenerator. Physical solvents, such as Purisol and Selexol, achieve much lower energy demands than this for pre-combustion capture, and hybrid processes of membranes and physical solvents have been reported elsewhere (26).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Post-Combustion
texts.text: Post-Combustion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The inclusion of the membrane gas separation stage upstream of a solvent process dramatically reduces the quantity of gas entering the absorber. The relative mass flowrate of the absorber inlet stream compared to the feed flowrate is provided in Fig. 3a as a function of membrane selectivity and pressure driving force. Increasing the membrane selectivity reduces the absorber inlet flow because CO2 is concentrated by the exclusion of N2. Even at the lowest selectivity and pressure driving force the flowrate to the solvent plant is reduced by 45% compared to the base case, while for a pressure driving force of 400 kPa and CO2 = N2 selectivity of 100, this flowrate is a quarter of the feed. This reduction in flowrate results in a decrease in the solvent absorber column diameter (Fig. 3b).
texts.text: The inclusion of the membrane gas separation stage upstream of a solvent process dramatically reduces the quantity of gas entering the absorber. The relative mass flowrate of the absorber inlet stream compared to the feed flowrate is provided in Fig. 3a as a function of membrane selectivity and pressure driving force. Increasing the membrane selectivity reduces the absorber inlet flow because CO2 is concentrated by the exclusion of N2. Even at the lowest selectivity and pressure driving force the flowrate to the solvent plant is reduced by 45% compared to the base case, while for a pressure driving force of 400 kPa and CO2 = N2 selectivity of 100, this flowrate is a quarter of the feed. This reduction in flowrate results in a decrease in the solvent absorber column diameter (Fig. 3b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The presence of the membrane also leads to a higher CO2 concentration in the inlet to the solvent process, as shown in Fig. 4. For the lowest selectivity and pressure driving force, the membrane effectively doubles the CO2 concentration. Increasing the pressure driving force across the membrane also results in a further increase in CO2 concentration. This increase in CO2 concentration enhances the driving force for mass transfer in the absorber, leading to a reduction in column height. For a CO2 = N2 selectivity of 40 and a 200 kPa pressure driving force, the column height is reduced to 12 m relative to a base case height of 26 m. This is an important finding as absorber height reduction is an important aspect of solvent research (27,28). The regenerator column dimensions change little,
texts.text: The presence of the membrane also leads to a higher CO2 concentration in the inlet to the solvent process, as shown in Fig. 4. For the lowest selectivity and pressure driving force, the membrane effectively doubles the CO2 concentration. Increasing the pressure driving force across the membrane also results in a further increase in CO2 concentration. This increase in CO2 concentration enhances the driving force for mass transfer in the absorber, leading to a reduction in column height. For a CO2 = N2 selectivity of 40 and a 200 kPa pressure driving force, the column height is reduced to 12 m relative to a base case height of 26 m. This is an important finding as absorber height reduction is an important aspect of solvent research (27,28). The regenerator column dimensions change little,

texts.orig: because the same amount of loaded MEA solvent passes through the system. Similar reductions in absorber size are expected for other solvent systems.
texts.text: because the same amount of loaded MEA solvent passes through the system. Similar reductions in absorber size are expected for other solvent systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a membrane permeability of 500 barrer, which is comparable to existing commercial membranes, the required membrane area is between 0.2 million and 1 million m 2 , depending on membrane selectivity (Fig. 5a). This membrane area is on a scale comparable to existing natural gas sweetening plants (3). Increasing membrane permeability reduces this membrane area, consistent with previously reported trends for post combustion membrane plants (9). Increasing the selectivity has a smaller effect, again consistent with previous work. The configuration of the membrane module also has a minor influence, with a cross-flow module requiring more membrane area than counter-current flow (13).
texts.text: For a membrane permeability of 500 barrer, which is comparable to existing commercial membranes, the required membrane area is between 0.2 million and 1 million m 2 , depending on membrane selectivity (Fig. 5a). This membrane area is on a scale comparable to existing natural gas sweetening plants (3). Increasing membrane permeability reduces this membrane area, consistent with previously reported trends for post combustion membrane plants (9). Increasing the selectivity has a smaller effect, again consistent with previous work. The configuration of the membrane module also has a minor influence, with a cross-flow module requiring more membrane area than counter-current flow (13).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CAPEX saving from the reduced absorber size is offset by the cost of the membrane module, compressor or blower and vacuum pump required. If the difference in CAPEX between the hybrid process and base case absorber columns is compared to the CAPEX associated with the membrane unit, then a membrane price can be determined that represents CAPEX equality for the two cases. If the actual membrane price is under this value, then the CAPEX of the hybrid process is less than the CAPEX of the base case. The membrane price needed for CAPEX equality is shown in Fig. 5b. The estimated cost ranges from 0.1 to 1000 US$ = m 2 , depending on CO2 permeability and pressure driving force. Operating a feed compressor in place of a blower and vacuum pump combination represents a 15-25% CAPEX increase, dependent on membrane selectivity. This is because of the difference in size between the vacuum pump and compressor, because the feed compressor must handle a high N2 content (15). However, this is partially offset by the lower membrane area required because of the increased pressure driving force. Using a membrane with a CAPEX below that depicted in Fig. 5b will enable a CAPEX saving for the hybrid plant. Both Baker and Lokhandwala (29) and Merkel et al (30) suggest that the cost of a membrane plant in natural gas processing is US$500 = m 2 . On a purely CAPEX basis, this means for a post-combustion scenario the membrane CO2 permeability must be at least 10,000 barrer. No membranes currently commercially available achieve this permeability. However in natural gas processing only part of the plant cost is due to the membrane, with module housings, connecting valves and the supporting structure also included in this cost. Baker and Lokhandwala (29) indicate that this price can be reduced considerably by using fiberglass vessels and plastic components, such as those used in the water industry, to a level of US$ 30-50 = m 2 . Under this scenario, the membrane CO2 permeability must be of the order of 1000 barrer to achieve a CAPEX saving. Many polymeric membranes have performances in this range (31), although none to date have been commercialized.

This is because of the difference in size between the vacuum pump and compressor, because the feed compressor must handle a high N2 content (15). Using a membrane with a CAPEX below that depicted in Fig. 5b will enable a CAPEX saving for the hybrid plant. On a purely CAPEX basis, this means for a post-combustion scenario the membrane CO2 permeability must be at least 10,000 barrer. No membranes currently commercially available achieve this permeability. Under this scenario, the membrane CO2 permeability must be of the order of 1000 barrer to achieve a CAPEX saving. Many polymeric membranes have performances in this range (31), although none to date have been commercialized.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy demand of the combined hybrid plant, relative to the base MEA case, is provided in Fig. 6. For all cases, the hybrid process is more energy intensive than the base case. This comes from the need to generate a pressure driving force across the membrane, which requires a significant electrical energy input to the feed blower = compressor and vacuum pump. Improving the selectivity of the membrane lowers this energy demand; as the permeate flowrate is reduced, leading to a smaller vacuum pump duty. Conversely, the regenerator reboiler duty in the solvent plant changes little, because the same amount of CO2 is regenerated for both designs. The cost of electrical energy is roughly three times the cost of

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Pre-Combustion
texts.text: Pre-Combustion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of a hydrogen selective membrane in a hybrid plant separates H2 into the permeate stream and concentrates CO2 in the retentate. For oxygen blown gasification with water-gas shift, the membrane can concentrate CO2 up to 85% in the retentate stream, and the solvent process is therefore separating a highly concentrated feed. This is beneficial for physical solvents, such as Purisol and Selexol, which are commonly used for CO2 separation from precombustion scenarios. The combination of a membrane unit with a physical solvent process have been discussed elsewhere (26). Alternatively, at such high CO2 concentrations and high pressure, other technologies such as cryogenic separation and adsorption may be more appropriate (2). However, in the present case, a reactive solvent, MEA, is used as the basis for comparison.
texts.text: The use of a hydrogen selective membrane in a hybrid plant separates H2 into the permeate stream and concentrates CO2 in the retentate. For oxygen blown gasification with water-gas shift, the membrane can concentrate CO2 up to 85% in the retentate stream, and the solvent process is therefore separating a highly concentrated feed. This is beneficial for physical solvents, such as Purisol and Selexol, which are commonly used for CO2 separation from precombustion scenarios. The combination of a membrane unit with a physical solvent process have been discussed elsewhere (26). Alternatively, at such high CO2 concentrations and high pressure, other technologies such as cryogenic separation and adsorption may be more appropriate (2). However, in the present case, a reactive solvent, MEA, is used as the basis for comparison.

texts.orig: The relative mass flowrate of the retentate to the feed is shown in Fig. 7. The decrease in flowrate is greater for the air-blown case, as some nitrogen permeates the membrane in addition to H2. This nitrogen loss means that the CO2 concentration in the feed to the absorber increases, from 0.25 to 0.37 as the membrane H2 = CO2 selectivity increases. In both cases the absorber column diameter and height decreases because of exclusion of H2. For the oxygen-blown case with a membrane H2 = CO2 selectivity of 10, the absorber diameter is 94% of the base case,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: versus Fig. 9a). When air-blown, this membrane area increases but is still considerably smaller than the postcombustion case (Fig. 5 versus Fig. 9b). The main issue with this membrane configuration is minimizing CO2 losses through the membrane, because of the low H2 = CO2 selectivities achievable with polymeric membranes. This is the limiting factor in the size of the membrane process, and as a consequence considerable H2 can remain in the retentate stream and enter the absorber. To ensure this H2 is not lost, the absorber exit gas, consisting of H2 and CO, must be recovered and reconnected with the membrane permeate stream before combustion. In addition, the permeate stream is recompressed from 2000 to 2500 kPaa, to enable comparison with the MEA base case.
texts.text: versus Fig. 9a). When air-blown, this membrane area increases but is still considerably smaller than the postcombustion case (Fig. 5 versus Fig. 9b). The main issue with this membrane configuration is minimizing CO2 losses through the membrane, because of the low H2 = CO2 selectivities achievable with polymeric membranes. This is the limiting factor in the size of the membrane process, and as a consequence considerable H2 can remain in the retentate stream and enter the absorber. To ensure this H2 is not lost, the absorber exit gas, consisting of H2 and CO, must be recovered and reconnected with the membrane permeate stream before combustion. In addition, the permeate stream is recompressed from 2000 to 2500 kPaa, to enable comparison with the MEA base case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the CAPEX to break even in the oxygen blown case, at a membrane cost of US$500 = m 2 , a H2 permeability of at least 500 barrer is needed, while at US 40 = m 2 , a permeability of between 10 and 100 barrer is required, Fig. 9c. Such permeabilities are readily attained for many reported membranes. Conversely, in the air blown case, CAPEX savings are harder to realize, because the absorber diameter reduction is not as great, which has a critical impact on absorber cost. For a membrane price of US$ 500 = m 2 , from a CAPEX saving perspective a H2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The additional energy demand of the H2-selective membrane pre-combustion process is less than 3%. The majority of this energy demand is associated with the recompression of the membrane permeate, which can be avoided if the downstream turbine operates at 2000 kPaa. Hence, it is possible for the hybrid process to have OPEX equality with the base case. The significant reduction in absorber height and small membrane area makes this hybrid process the most appealing, of the different scenarios studied, particularly in the oxygen blown case. Furthermore, the benefits of this design will also be present if the MEA solvent is changed to a physical solvent, such as Purisol or Selexol. This is because the increased CO2 concentration of the solvent feed gas also improves the driving force for absorption into physical solvents. Hence, a similar reduction in absorber height would be observed.
texts.text: The additional energy demand of the H2-selective membrane pre-combustion process is less than 3%. The majority of this energy demand is associated with the recompression of the membrane permeate, which can be avoided if the downstream turbine operates at 2000 kPaa. Hence, it is possible for the hybrid process to have OPEX equality with the base case. The significant reduction in absorber height and small membrane area makes this hybrid process the most appealing, of the different scenarios studied, particularly in the oxygen blown case. Furthermore, the benefits of this design will also be present if the MEA solvent is changed to a physical solvent, such as Purisol or Selexol. This is because the increased CO2 concentration of the solvent feed gas also improves the driving force for absorption into physical solvents. Hence, a similar reduction in absorber height would be observed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: from the stand alone individual capture technology processes. However, for each of the scenarios considered, the relative energy demand of the combined hybrid process was always greater than the base MEA solvent only comparison. This increase in energy was substantial for the post-combustion case, but less than a 3% increase for the pre-combustion scenarios.
texts.text: from the stand alone individual capture technology processes. However, for each of the scenarios considered, the relative energy demand of the combined hybrid process was always greater than the base MEA solvent only comparison. This increase in energy was substantial for the post-combustion case, but less than a 3% increase for the pre-combustion scenarios.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The post-combustion and H2-selective pre-combustion hybrid scenarios all reduced the solvent absorber height and diameter and thus gave a significant capital cost reduction for this piece of equipment. This CAPEX saving must be offset against the capital costs for the membrane plant and the additional compressor = vacuum pump required. The viability of such hybrid processes is thus heavily dependent upon the cost of this equipment and specifically the membrane plant. In turn, this cost is a strong function of the membrane selectivity, as this dictates the membrane area. Future work should focus on the development of more selective membranes; while also reducing the cost per square metre of such installations.
texts.text: The post-combustion and H2-selective pre-combustion hybrid scenarios all reduced the solvent absorber height and diameter and thus gave a significant capital cost reduction for this piece of equipment. This CAPEX saving must be offset against the capital costs for the membrane plant and the additional compressor = vacuum pump required. The viability of such hybrid processes is thus heavily dependent upon the cost of this equipment and specifically the membrane plant. In turn, this cost is a strong function of the membrane selectivity, as this dictates the membrane area. Future work should focus on the development of more selective membranes; while also reducing the cost per square metre of such installations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most prospective case investigated was clearly the use of a H2-selective membrane downstream of an oxygen blown gasifier. This scenario required a smaller membrane area than the other hybrid plants, and resulted in reductions in the height of the solvent absorber. The increase in energy demand was also trivial. This makes it easier for this hybrid process to achieve a positive cost saving compared to the MEA base case, although as above, this will depend upon the development of low cost, high selectivity membrane materials. This configuration should be the focus of future investigations.
texts.text: The most prospective case investigated was clearly the use of a H2-selective membrane downstream of an oxygen blown gasifier. This scenario required a smaller membrane area than the other hybrid plants, and resulted in reductions in the height of the solvent absorber. The increase in energy demand was also trivial. This makes it easier for this hybrid process to achieve a positive cost saving compared to the MEA base case, although as above, this will depend upon the development of low cost, high selectivity membrane materials. This configuration should be the focus of future investigations.
